skill.name: accessibility-fundamentals
skill.description: Review JSX, forms, navigation, and interactive components for WCAG-aligned accessibility issues.
skill.summary: Guides accessibility reviews for JSX interfaces, forms, buttons, modals, and navigation.
skill.author: DanielPodolsky

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer matches for Ruby or shell execution, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance are false positives in Markdown accessibility examples. The skill contains guidance text, JSX snippets, CSS snippets, ARIA references, and optional documentation search notes, with no executable scripts or prompt injection attempts found.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Analyzer Matches
low_findings.description: The reported external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns appear inside Markdown prose, JSX examples, CSS examples, ARIA attribute names, or documentation search notes. No evidence found of executable command invocation, cryptographic implementation, system probing, data exfiltration, or malicious intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is a Markdown skill guide and the cited sections are instructional accessibility examples. There are no runnable scripts, shell command blocks, network endpoints, or secret-handling instructions.

content.value_statement: Interactive UI code often ships with hidden accessibility defects. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused checklist for WCAG-aligned JSX reviews.
actual_capabilities: Reviews JSX buttons, links, forms, navigation, modals, and custom interactive components.
Checks for keyboard access, visible focus states, semantic HTML, labels, alt text, and contrast concerns.
Explains common accessibility anti-patterns with safer replacement guidance.
Provides React-focused examples for accessible names, form errors, and ARIA attributes.
Suggests manual checks and automated testing with axe-style accessibility assertions.
limitations: Does not run browser-based accessibility scans by itself.
Does not guarantee full legal compliance with accessibility regulations.
Requires the user to provide UI code, screenshots, or component behavior for review.
Focuses mainly on JSX and React-style interfaces.

use_cases.title: Review a React Pull Request
use_cases.description: Identify accessibility defects in new buttons, forms, dialogs, and navigation before merge.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Design Handoff
use_cases.description: Check whether planned components include labels, focus states, contrast, and keyboard behavior.

use_cases.title: Improve Code Review Quality
use_cases.description: Use consistent accessibility questions when mentoring developers or reviewing UI changes.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Component Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this JSX component for accessibility issues. Focus on semantic elements, labels, keyboard access, and visible focus.

prompt_templates.title: Form Accessibility Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this form for accessible labels, error messages, required fields, keyboard flow, and screen reader announcements.

prompt_templates.title: Modal Interaction Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this modal or menu interaction for focus trapping, escape behavior, ARIA state, tab order, and announcements.

prompt_templates.title: Full UI Accessibility Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a structured accessibility review of this UI flow. Prioritize WCAG risks, user impact, fixes, and test coverage.

output_examples.input: A delete button shows only an icon and has no visible text.
output: Issue: The control may not have an accessible name.
Impact: Screen reader users may hear an unclear button announcement.
Suggested fix: Add a clear accessible label and hide decorative icon content from assistive technology.

output_examples.input: A form uses placeholders as the only field labels.
output: Issue: Placeholder text is not a reliable label.
Impact: Users can lose context after typing or when using assistive technology.
Suggested fix: Add persistent labels and connect each label to its input.

output_examples.input: A modal opens but focus remains behind the dialog.
output: Issue: Keyboard focus is not managed when the dialog opens.
Impact: Keyboard users may interact with hidden or inactive page content.
Suggested fix: Move focus into the dialog, trap focus, and restore focus when it closes.

best_practices: Review accessibility while code is still small and easy to change.
Combine semantic HTML with manual keyboard and screen reader checks.
Treat automated accessibility tests as a baseline, not complete coverage.
anti_patterns: Using clickable div elements instead of native buttons or links.
Removing focus outlines without providing an equally visible replacement.
Using color alone to communicate errors, status, or selected states.

faq.question: Does this skill run accessibility tests?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and testing suggestions. You must run the tools in your own project.

faq.question: Is this skill only for React?
faq.answer: It is most specific for JSX and React patterns, but many checklist items apply to other web interfaces.

faq.question: Can it certify WCAG compliance?
faq.answer: No. It can help find issues, but certification requires broader testing and qualified review.

faq.question: What accessibility topics does it emphasize?
faq.answer: It emphasizes keyboard use, focus visibility, semantic HTML, form labels, alt text, ARIA, and contrast.

faq.question: Can designers use it?
faq.answer: Yes. Designers can use it to review interaction requirements, labels, focus states, and color-dependent cues.

faq.question: Does it replace manual testing?
faq.answer: No. Manual keyboard, zoom, and screen reader testing remain important for real user experience.
